behaviors.description: The generated reply references specific points from the incoming email rather than producing a generic response
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends entirely on whether the LLM prompt structure actually drives context-aware responses. A mocked LLM would always return contextually appropriate text (mock returns whatever we program), hiding real failures where the prompt is poorly engineered and the real LLM produces generic summaries despite the instruction. Only a real LLM call with a real email thread can demonstrate that the prompt engineering actually causes the model to reference specific details like names, account numbers, or particular issues mentioned in the email.
behaviors.test_description: Feed a real email thread with specific, testable details (e.g., 'Customer Sarah reported issue X affecting account 12345 since Tuesday') into the draft generation system. Make a real LLM call with the context-awareness prompt. Verify the generated draft explicitly references at least 2-3 specific details from the email (names, numbers, specific problems) rather than using generic language like 'following up on your issue'. This catches failures where the prompt doesn't effectively instruct the LLM to be context-specific, or where the model ignores context instructions.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test requires real LLM infrastructure to verify that the prompt structure and model behavior together produce context-aware responses. Mocking would defeat the purpose.

behaviors.description: The factual consistency check flags claims in the draft that contradict entries in the knowledge base
behaviors.justification: The core failure boundary is whether the system can query a real KB and apply contradiction detection logic. A unit test with a mocked KB and mocked semantic checker would hide real failures: incorrect KB schema assumptions, failed queries, or logic errors in how results are processed. However, the contradiction detection algorithm itself could use keyword/rule matching testable against mocked semantic similarity at Integration level. If the system uses true semantic embeddings (likely in realistic implementations), this becomes Agentic, but the minimum is Integration to catch schema and query failures against real KB.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a real knowledge base file or database with a specific, verifiable fact (e.g., 'Product X costs $100', 'Policy Y requires 48-hour notice'). Generate a draft that explicitly contradicts this (e.g., 'Product X is $50' or 'Policy Y requires 24-hour notice'). Run the fact-checking module with real KB access and verify it flags the contradiction. Then test a non-contradictory claim in the draft and verify it is not flagged. This catches failures in KB queries, schema mismatches, contradiction detection thresholds, and false-positive/false-negative rates.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Requires real KB to test actual query behavior and schema handling. Rule-based contradiction detection can be tested at Integration; if semantic similarity is core, upgrade to Agentic.

behaviors.description: When the knowledge base has no relevant entries, the draft includes a disclaimer rather than fabricating facts
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires verifying that a real LLM respects an instruction to include a disclaimer when the KB is empty. A mocked LLM would always include the disclaimer (because we programmed the mock to do so), hiding real failures where the live model ignores the instruction or fabricates facts despite being told not to. Only a real LLM call can show whether the model actually follows the 'include disclaimer when KB is empty' instruction in the prompt.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the KB lookup to return zero relevant entries for a question. Make a real LLM call with the draft generation prompt (which should include an instruction like 'If the knowledge base has no relevant entries, include a disclaimer stating "This answer is based on general knowledge, not verified company data"'). Verify the real LLM output includes a clear disclaimer or caveat rather than fabricated specifics. Test edge cases: partial KB hits (some entries found, some not) and completely absent topics. This catches failures where the LLM ignores the disclaimer instruction or fabricates details despite the explicit instruction not to.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test requires a real LLM to verify it respects the disclaimer instruction. Mocking would always pass even if the real LLM ignores the instruction.

behaviors.description: The approval workflow blocks sending until the user explicitly approves — no draft is sent automatically
behaviors.justification: This is pure control flow logic: the approval gate must block sending if approval is not given, and enable sending if approval is given. No external infrastructure, LLM, or multi-step orchestration is required. Mock the email sending and user approval functions, then verify the gate's control flow logic. A mocked send_email function will clearly show whether it is or is not called in each scenario, catching logic errors like always-sending, wrong conditions, or missing approval checks.
behaviors.test_description: Create a mock send_email function that records whether it was called. In test case 1: invoke the approval workflow without user approval and verify send_email was never called. In test case 2: invoke after simulated user approval (mock approve_user() to return true) and verify send_email is called exactly once. In test case 3: verify approval is required—attempt to send before showing approval UI and confirm sending is blocked. Test edge cases: multiple approval prompts, canceling approval after initial approval, rapid re-submissions. This catches logic errors in the approval gate (off-by-one conditions, inverted boolean checks, missing approval calls).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: This is pure control flow with no external dependencies. Mocking send_email and approval functions is sufficient; no need for real email, LLM, or KB. Unit level correctly captures the minimum.

behaviors.description: The full sequence — read thread, generate draft, check facts, present for approval — works end-to-end for a real email thread
behaviors.justification: This behavior explicitly requires testing the full pipeline in sequence with real components. It involves at least two agentic steps: draft generation (LLM call) and fact-checking (likely LLM-based semantic comparison). Mocking the LLM would hide prompt-chain failures, KB integration issues, or semantic contradiction detection bugs. Real email thread input is required to test email parsing, thread reconstruction, and context preservation across the pipeline. The approval gate must be integrated into the workflow. Only Workflow level—multiple agentic steps in real sequence—can catch integration failures like KB access from within the pipeline, LLM prompt chain issues, or approval gate integration bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Create or load a realistic multi-message email thread (e.g., customer support conversation with 5-10 messages) containing: specific details (customer name, account ID, problem description), facts that should be checked (e.g., 'we promised 48-hour resolution'), and edge cases (contradictory statements in the thread, references to external context). Run the full pipeline: (1) parse the email thread, (2) generate a draft reply using real LLM with context-aware prompt, (3) run fact-checking against real KB, (4) present the draft for approval with all fact-check flags visible, (5) simulate user approval and verify sending succeeds. Verify: each stage produces the expected output type (structured draft with metadata), fact-checks catch real contradictions from step 1, approval gate blocks until approval is given, and final sent email contains all expected parts. Test failure modes: malformed email threads, KB service failures during the pipeline, LLM timeouts, and approval cancellation mid-workflow.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The behavior explicitly tests the full sequence 'end-to-end', requiring all components (real email, real LLM, real KB, approval gate) wired together. Mocking any component would hide integration failures. Multiple agentic steps in real sequence require Workflow level.
